name: Cetina Canyon Zipline (Omiš)
description: 8-line zipline over the Cetina canyon near Omiš — 2,100m total, longest line 700m at 150m above the canyon floor. The crowd-pleaser of the coast's adventure hub; office in the old town, course ~3km upstream.
communityNotes: Consistently praised on TripAdvisor: ‘The views are phenomenal... good value for money especially since you do several zip lines’ (Apr 2026); ‘nothing compares to the adrenaline rush, the views’ (May 2026). Operated by Zip-line Croatia; most ‘from Split’ tours resell this same course.
cost: €80 pp (private €120); book ≥1 day ahead in June

name: Cetina Canyoning (Zadvarje / Gubavica)
description: Canyoning in the Cetina gorge near Zadvarje — the most adventurous Cetina activity. The Extreme tour has cliff jumps up to ~12m and rope rappels (one near the 60m Gubavica waterfall); every jump/rappel has a walk-around.
communityNotes: 'A mix of fun and adventure – swimming in clear water, jumping off rocks, sliding down waterfalls' (Sep 2025). Guides singled out by name — Vladimir (Dal Mare) and Malduk's Mountain Rescue–credentialed team. Book 1–2 days ahead in June (fills fast). Dal Mare direct: canyoning-cetina.com (~€70pp Extreme). Malduk: maldukadventures.com.
cost: Extreme ~€70 pp (4 × ~€280); Basic ~€50 pp
bestTime: Book Jun 17 morning slot. June = good water. Extreme is 18+.

name: Radmanove Mlinice (Cetina canyon / rafting finish)
description: 18th-century watermill restaurant deep in the Cetina canyon ~6km upstream of Omiš, and the classic finish point for Cetina rafting. Shaded riverside konoba — a good lunch + swim stop.
communityNotes: Cetina rafting (mostly Class I–II with a couple of III drops, ~9–12km, 3–4h) is the tame-but-scenic option vs canyoning: 'a beautiful tranquil place with awesome scenery... turtles and a lot of bird life.' Rafting ~€45 pp incl. minibus transfer (Rafting Cetina, 2024–25); optional ~5m cave-roof jump.
cost: Rafting ~€45 pp; restaurant à la carte

name: Starigrad (Fortica) Fortress, Omiš
description: 15th-c. fortress on a 262m cliff above Omiš — the higher of the town's two forts, with a vertigo-inducing panorama over the town, sea and Cetina canyon mouth. The in-town Mirabela tower is the quick 5-min alternative.
communityNotes: 'Quite challenging... at the top the views are fabulous and vertigo-inducing... about 45 minutes to reach the top' (bbqboy trip report). Two routes up — shaded river side (also a via ferrata start) or sunnier sea side. Note: a Google pin labelled ‘Starigrad Trailhead’ actually leads to Mirabela.
cost: €3.50 pp (cash)
bestTime: early morning or late afternoon — no shade

name: Auto Camp Lisičina (Omiš)
description: Small family-run campsite right by Omiš old town and the Cetina river — walkable to everything, tucked behind rocks so it's calm. Entrance is a narrow gap between boulders (fine for tents/cars).
communityNotes: park4night: 'Lovely small site. Entrance looks scary but it's easier than it looks... €32 per night'; 'Sehr netter und sauberer Campingplatz... sehr freundliche Betreiber.' Cash only.
bestTime: Book by email (kamp-lisicina@inet.hr) or phone (+385 95 5123 473) — no online booking. Night 2 (Jun 17).
cost: ~€30–40/night for 4 + tent + car (cash only)
facilities: Warm showers, clean sanitary block, electricity, WiFi, drinking water, laundry

name: Kašjuni Beach (Marjan, Split)
description: Long pebble cove under Marjan hill — the clearest water in Split and the locals' pick over crowded Bačvice. Pine shade behind, a beach bar on site, great at sunset.
communityNotes: 'Consistently ranked among the best swimming spots in the city... clear turquoise water and far fewer tourists than Bačvice.' Caveats: gets busy/Instagrammy, steep walk down, parking fills early, little shade after morning.
bestTime: morning for shade/parking; sunset for the light

name: Marjan – Vidilica viewpoint (Split)
description: First viewpoint on Marjan hill, ~15min up the stairs from the Varoš/old-town side, with a café at the top. Big payoff for little effort — city, harbour and Adriatic; best at sunset. The full Marjan summit (Telegrin) is a longer forest walk.
communityNotes: 'Vidilica viewpoint delivers excellent views... quick city views without needing to hike far.' The easy, high-value Split add-on once you've wandered Diocletian's Palace.
bestTime: sunset

name: Split nightlife (Old Town bars + Vanilla)
description: Split's nightlife core: old-town bars (Charlie's, Fabrique) warming up to clubs — Central in the centre, Vanilla by Poljud stadium. A fun mainland night out and the realistic party window on the Zadar→Dubrovnik leg, though a notch below Hvar for clubbing.
communityNotes: Recurring advice: do an old-town bar crawl (Charlie's → Vanilla, often with free club entry via pub crawls) rather than the Bačvice mega-clubs, where reviewers flag pricey cover (€30–50) and aggressive bouncers. Charlie's is the most-loved spot (4.8/5, cheap drinks, young international crowd).
bestTime: bars to ~1am, then clubs (verify venue names closer to 2026 — scene churns)

name: Trogir Old Town
description: Compact UNESCO medieval old town on a little island 30min west of Split — Romanesque-Gothic lanes, St Lawrence cathedral (climb the bell tower), Kamerlengo fortress. A pretty 1.5–2h leg-stretch + lunch stop, not a base.
communityNotes: 'A very charming pretty town but very small... worth seeing if you have time.' Kamerlengo fortress (~€5) splits opinion: 'the view from the top is absolutely stunning' vs 'not worth the entrance fee... dilapidated... no information inside' — pay only for the rooftop panorama. Evenings are quiet.

name: Nugal Beach (hidden cove, Makarska)
description: Pine-fringed pebble cove ~20–30min walk (unsigned) south of Makarska along the coastal path, or by boat — the riviera's prettiest ‘secret’. Historically nudist, now mixed; no facilities, bring water.
communityNotes: 'It takes about 30 minutes from the harbor. However, it is not signposted' (Aug 2025); 'Great beach... good space for everyone' (Jun 2025); 'go there early because there will be more people as the day goes on.' Crystal-clear water, good snorkelling.
bestTime: go early

name: Biokovo NP scenic toll road (booking + drive)
description: The Biokovo Nature Park toll road — 23km of asphalt switchbacks from the Podgora gate up to the Skywalk and Sveti Jure (1762m), Croatia's highest road. Single-lane two-way with frequent lay-bys; doable in a rental for a calm driver. (Skywalk + Sveti Jure are pinned separately.)
communityNotes: 'You need not be worried at all... I don't remember ever having to reverse' (Croatia forum expert); 'We arrived around 7:30am and basically drove to the top without any trouble. Coming down... you often have to pull over' (bbqboy). Avoid in fog.
cost: €15/adult Jun–Sep (~€60 for 4); under-7 free
timeNeeded: ~30min to Skywalk, ~1h to Sveti Jure; 3–4h round trip
bestTime: BOOK Jun 18 morning slot now at shop.pp-biokovo.hr — capped at 20 cars/hour; sells out in June. Open 06:00–20:00.

name: Makarska (Deep cave club + Osejava cliffs)
description: Makarska is the riviera's night-out + cliff-jumping base: ‘Deep’ nightclub carved into a sea cave on the Osejava peninsula (daily ~21:00–05:00), waterfront bars on the Riva (Pub Pivac, Buba Beach Bar), and a cliffy cape with clear-water cliff jumps.
communityNotes: The Osejava cape is 'an entire cliffy coast full of cliff jumps and mind-blowingly clear water' (check depth before jumping). The cave club makes Makarska the obvious nightlife stop on the Zadar→Dubrovnik leg if you camp on the riviera.

name: Pelješac Bridge (Dubrovnik approach)
description: The 2.4km Pelješac Bridge (opened 2022) bypasses the Neum/Bosnia corridor entirely, keeping the Split↔Dubrovnik drive 100% in Croatia/Schengen — no border stop, no toll. The recommended, reliable late-afternoon approach before the airport pickup.
communityNotes: 'Smooth, well signposted, and surprisingly quick... turned out to be a highlight of the trip.' Neum is only ~12min shorter, and only if there's no queue — summer border waits run up to ~2h each way. Bridge renovation doesn't start until Oct 2026, so it's fully open in June 2026. ⚡ No dedicated stop needed — you drive over it; it's a logistics pin, not a destination.

name: Mljet National Park (the salt lakes)
description: Two connected saltwater lakes (Veliko & Malo Jezero) ringed by pine, with a Benedictine monastery on St Mary islet (free electric boat from Pristanište), the Montokuc viewpoint and a flat cycling/walking loop. Swim, kayak, bike.
communityNotes: Car ferry lands at Sobra ~30min from the NP, so an overnight beats a rushed day trip: 'doing both ends of the island in one day is very tight... have a night on Mljet (I'd choose Polače).' Entry is steep — €25/adult in June (~€100 for 4), confirmed by the official 2026 price list (PDF adopted Oct 30 2025). Precisely: the −25% after 16:00 applies to adult/child/student tickets bought AT the park; the St Mary boat, parking and electric train are included in the ticket.
cost: €25/adult June (−25% after 16:00, bought at the park; boat/parking/train included); Prapratno↔Sobra ferry car+4 ~€50
bestTime: overnight recommended when you bring the car

name: Odysseus Cave (Mljet, cliff-jump swim)
description: Collapsed sea cave with an emerald pool open to the Adriatic near Babino Polje (eastern Mljet, ~10min from Sobra ferry) — free, with cliff-jump ledges and a swim-through. The approach is tougher than blogs suggest: sharp, jagged karst with steep, wobbly final sections — up to ~1h down with breaks, not a uniform 20–30 min — and the cave interior is swim-in only. Water shoes mandatory.
communityNotes: 'Multiple cliff jumping points... from 2–3m to 5m+ and 10m+.' 'Find the spot with a rope anchored onto the rocks' for the exit. Go in the morning — 'around 12:00 there's more people.' Perfect to do the afternoon you land, before driving to the NP side. Park at Tommy's Supermarket in Babino Polje then follow the marked trail (~20 min). Best cave light 09:00–11:00. Cave is technically outside the NP boundary — free even without a park ticket. (Coord derived from DMS, medium confidence.)
bestTime: morning; bring water shoes
cost: Free (outside NP boundary — no ticket needed)

name: Autokamp Marina (Ropa, Mljet)
description: Small, quiet, tent-friendly campsite at Ropa in central Mljet — the closest camp to the NP side. ~10 olive-shaded terraced pitches; rocky beach a 10–15min steep walk down.
communityNotes: park4night: 'Very quiet auto camp and friendly staff. Basic but okay facilities'; 'camping under olive trees, very friendly and accommodating.' Hot showers, shared kitchen, electricity (Schuko adapter).
cost: ~€28/night for 2 + van (+€6 electricity); budget ~€35–45 for 4 + tent
facilities: Hot showers, shared kitchen + fridge, electricity, olive shade; max vehicle ~6.5m

name: Korčula Old Town
description: ‘Mini-Dubrovnik’ — a tiny fortified Venetian old town on a peninsula with a herringbone street plan, Marco Polo legend, the Moreška sword dance and a climbable tower. A 2h wander + meal; the island's real draw is its beaches and Lumbarda wine.
communityNotes: 'If you value history, natural beauty, local food, and a genuine island atmosphere, Korčula is absolutely worth visiting.' The Marco Polo link is legend, not fact — treat the ‘house’ as a photo stop. Moreška sword dance: Thu 21:00 in June if you want to catch it.

name: Pupnatska Luka beach (Korčula)
description: Korčula's signature beach — a deep horseshoe bay of pale pebbles ringed by pines, water turning turquoise within ~10m. ~25–30min down a winding road from Korčula town.
communityNotes: 'The breathtaking scenery and tranquil atmosphere make it absolutely worth the visit despite the parking fee.' 'The drive down is scenic but narrow... blind corners.' Car park ~€10 cash, sunbeds ~€25; pebble — bring water shoes; only really crowded in August.
bestTime: before midday for parking

name: Walls of Ston (Pelješac)
description: The ‘European Wall of China’ — 5.5km of 14th-c. defensive walls climbing the hills between Ston and Mali Ston, the longest in Europe after China's. A real climb with steps and little shade; right at the foot of the Pelješac drive from Dubrovnik.
communityNotes: 'A small fee of €10 per person... very far from the exorbitant prices in Dubrovnik (€35).' 'Hiking the entire walls... at a leisurely pace took about three hours.' Walk it in the cooler morning/evening — midday is brutal.
bestTime: morning/evening — no shade

name: Mali Ston oysters
description: Mali Ston bay is Europe's most famous oyster ground (PDO Ostrea edulis). Eat them shucked-to-order on the waterfront at Bota Šare or Kapetanova kuća, or cheaper at roadside farm stalls — minutes off the Pelješac road.
communityNotes: 'The oysters are really fished out of the sea in front of you, opened and laid out for you.' ~€2–2.50/oyster at restaurants (cheaper at stalls), ~€25 pp for a few fresh ones + wine. Bota Šare is 'more laid back... fresh and tasty at half the price' of Kapetanova kuća.
bestTime: Day 3 (Jun 18) lunch stop en route Split→Dubrovnik. Bota Šare opens 12:00 — call ahead: +385 20 754-482. Off-peak = quicker service.
cost: ~€2–2.5 per oyster

name: Žuljana beach (Pelješac)
description: One of Pelješac's best beaches — a large pebble bay with very clear water, good for snorkelling/diving, on the peninsula's south coast. A small campsite sits at the bay; few facilities beyond the village.
communityNotes: Repeatedly named among Pelješac's most beautiful beaches by Croatian travel sources; family-shallow and quiet outside peak. Pairs well with a Dingač winery stop on the drive in.

name: Matuško winery (Potomje, Dingač)
description: Big, popular Plavac Mali producer in Potomje at the heart of the Dingač zone — the most walk-in-friendly of Pelješac's famous wineries, with a classic cellar tour + tasting. Drive the scenic Dingač vineyard road/tunnel above Trstenik.
communityNotes: 'You cannot fail in any [Pelješac winery], just try to visit a small family winery to get this ‘special touch.’' Matuško is the easy walk-in; Grgić (Trstenik, of Napa fame) for the view; Saints Hills / Korta Katarina are nicer but book ahead. Designate a driver.

name: Mt Srđ + Fort Imperial (Dubrovnik)
description: The 412m hill behind Dubrovnik with the best panorama in the city (old-town rooftops, Lokrum, the Elaphiti on the horizon) and Fort Imperial's Homeland War Museum. Cable car, or a free steep shadeless serpentine hike.
communityNotes: 'The views from the trail were some of the best of our visit.' Cable car €27 return (book online to skip cruise-day queues); hike ~45–60min, free — do it late afternoon, not midday. Museum ~€5. Be up ~45min before sunset for the light.
cost: Cable car €27 return; hike free; museum ~€5
bestTime: sunset; hike late afternoon

name: Buža Bar (Dubrovnik cliff bar)
description: Hole-in-the-wall cliff bar outside Dubrovnik's south wall, facing Lokrum — the city's sunset-drinks institution, with cliff-jumping ledges into the Adriatic below. STATUS UNCERTAIN for June 2026: the bar is in concession/legal limbo (Tourist Board: 'unable to provide a specific opening date'), and a May 2026 TripAdvisor review reports tables and chairs removed. The terrace and jump ledges stay physically accessible and free — fine as a sunset walk-by. RE-CHECK ON ARRIVAL; re-promote instantly if it's confirmed pouring drinks.
communityNotes: 'Walk through a hole in the city walls to find it... on the edge of a cliff staring directly at the Adriatic... one of the best views of Lokrum.' Cliff jumps realistically ~3–10m (higher figures unverified — check water/rocks first). Get there 2–3h before sunset in peak season for a spot — IF it's operating; Dubrovnik's concession litigation wave makes a normal season anything but guaranteed.
bestTime: before sunset; cash only; check operating status on arrival

name: Sveti Jakov beach (Dubrovnik)
description: Local-favourite pebble beach SE of the old town — the same walls-and-Lokrum view as Banje but far quieter, because ~150 stairs down filter the crowds. Sunset-facing, laid-back tiki bar, cheaper loungers.
communityNotes: 'A local favorite that isn't as well-known as Banje, but offers the same splendid views... more secluded, probably due to the over 150 stairs.' The effort is the filter — worth it for a calmer swim near town vs the crowded Banje beach club.
bestTime: afternoon/sunset

name: Lokrum island (Dubrovnik)
description: Forested nature-reserve island 10min by boat from the old-town harbour: roaming peacocks, a warm saltwater ‘Dead Sea’ lake with cliff-jumping, a Benedictine monastery + botanical garden, Fort Royal viewpoint and a Game of Thrones throne. The best cliff jumping near Dubrovnik.
communityNotes: 'Lokrum is a great place to go cliff diving, with cliffs starting at a couple meters and going up to 10 meters.' Round-trip boat €30 incl. reserve entry, every ~30min. NOTE: protected reserve — no camping/overnight, everyone off on the last evening boat.
cost: €30 round-trip boat (incl. entry)

name: Šunj beach, Lopud (Elaphiti)
description: Šunj beach on car-free Lopud — the only proper sandy beach in the Dubrovnik area: a wide south-facing crescent of shallow warm water, ~20min forest walk from the village. Jadrolinija ferry from Gruž (~1h); no driving needed.
communityNotes: 'If you are looking for a nice sand beach, I would say Šunj beach on Lopud.' Timing matters: 'at ten it's nearly empty... by one the ferry has deposited two hundred people' — take an early ferry, leave before the 1pm crowd. June/Sept are best.
bestTime: early ferry, off by ~1pm

name: Koločep & Blue Cave (Elaphiti)
description: Koločep, the closest Elaphiti island to Dubrovnik, and its Blue Cave (Modra špilja) — a sea cave that glows electric blue, reachable only by swimming/kayak/boat. Quiet beaches and a couple of bars; the highlight of most boat/kayak day-tours.
communityNotes: 'The Blue Cave can only be accessed by swimming... sunlight reflecting through creates a magical blue glow.' For an adventurous group the strongest combo is a kayak/boat tour pairing Koločep's Blue Cave with Lopud's Šunj beach in one day.

name: Konoba Feral — Brijesta, Pelješac (on-route seafood)
description: Father-in-law fishes daily, wife cooks. On the D415 south-coast Pelješac road between Ston and Orebić/Korčula ferry — fits perfectly on the Jun 18 drive or the Mljet/Pelješac day. Homemade pasta, grilled catch, local wine. ~€25-30pp. 4.7/5 across 600+ reviews.
communityNotes: Small family restaurant — call ahead (they have limited seating). Genuine konoba feel with no tourist-trap pricing. Sits on the less-traveled south coast road of Pelješac which most people skip in favor of the main road — reward for taking the scenic route. Pair with a Potomje village wine stop 15km before Orebić.
bestTime: call ahead; lunch or early dinner

name: Cape Planka (Rt Planka) — New Moon Milky Way Jun 16-18
description: Headland ~30-40 min from Split, 15-20 min walk to the lighthouse. Open Adriatic to the south with no island light pollution, light pollution behind you. Confirmed astrophotography spot. The new moon falls Jun 15 — the first 3 nights of the trip (Jun 16-18) are the darkest sky window of the entire journey.
communityNotes: Cape Planka is the clearest Adriatic-facing dark sky on the route. Access: drive to the cape parking, 15-20 min trail to the lighthouse area. No curfew or access restrictions. Best window: Jun 16-18 (new moon Jun 15 = nearly no moonlight). After Jun 20, waxing crescent starts washing out the Milky Way core. The Milky Way core rises in the south and east — face away from the coast lights behind you.
bestTime: Jun 17 evening from Omiš/Makarska base (~50km/45min). Jun 16 from Paklenica = 2h round-trip at 22:00 (too much on Day 1). Arrive 21:00, Milky Way core peaks 23:00-01:00.
cost: Free

name: Virgin Beach (Djevičanska plaža) — Drašnice/Igrane
description: 1km of unspoiled pebble beach with freshwater springs, between Drašnice and Igrane on the Makarska Riviera. Access via old unpaved track (passable in the Sportage). Very few tourists know this route. Free, no loungers, no facilities.
communityNotes: Genuinely hidden — the access track is unmarked. Head to Drašnice village, follow signs toward the sea past the campsite, track continues on gravel ~500m. Freshwater springs make the water unusually cold (refreshing in summer heat). The beach itself is wide enough to spread out even in July.

name: Gubavica Waterfall (Cetina canyon)
description: 53m waterfall dropping into the Cetina canyon, 5min walk from roadside parking on the Makarska inland corridor. Free, no facilities — a 30min stop that punches well above its time cost.
communityNotes: Viewpoint is free and immediately accessible. The pool at the base is for canyoners with gear — don't try to descend without a guide. But the falls themselves are stunning from the viewpoint. On the Makarska→Omiš inland road near Zadvarje. Easy add-on to the Cetina/Omiš day.
cost: Free

name: Autokamp Lovor (Kozarica, Mljet)
description: Terraced campsite on the northern coast of Mljet at Kozarica village, 15km from the Sobra ferry port. The most atmospheric camp on the island — olive and pine shade, small boat ramp, 24h reception. Hot showers, electricity, laundry, fridge rental. Note: the approach road is narrow — reviewers flag it as tight for large vans. This is the '~€72' camp referenced in the route skeleton.
communityNotes: park4night 4.31/5, 26 reviews (July 2025): 'helpful owner Ana,' clean facilities. Complained: loud frogs, hard road for big vehicles. High season Jun 16–Sep 15. Book ahead — it fills. | Separate from Autokamp Marina (Ropa, cheaper, smaller) — do not confuse.
cost: ~€67–77 for 4 + tent + car (June high season; adult €10 + pitch ~€12; electricity +€7)
facilities: hot showers, toilets, electricity, laundry, fridge rental, boat ramp, 24h reception

name: Autokamp Mungos (Babino Polje, Mljet)
description: Largest campsite on Mljet (200–250 capacity) at Babino Polje near Odysseus Cave and Sutmiholjska beach — restaurant, bar, mini-market, pool, rent-a-bike/scooter, WiFi, exchange office. The most resort-style option on the island. Season May 20–Sep 20.
communityNotes: camping.info 3.8/5: basic but clean, friendly staff, convenient for the beach and cave. Best option if you want amenities over character. Larger groups find it easier to get a pitch here than at Marina or Lovor.
cost: ~€38–50 for 4 + tent + car (June; exact per-person pricing — call ahead)
facilities: restaurant, bar, mini-market, pool, rent-a-bike, wifi, exchange office

name: Camping Kate (Mlini, near Dubrovnik)
description: Small family-run campsite on the north edge of Mlini village, 10km south of Dubrovnik. 40 pitches on two terraces among old cypresses; pebble beach below via 160+ steps. Best base for Dubrovnik day trips — Bus #10 stop is 200m away, last bus back 23:50 weekdays / 01:30 weekends.
communityNotes: park4night 4.42/5 (552 reviews): 'Perfectly located for Dubrovnik visits'; 'very clean toilets and showers'; 'fills up by early afternoon in peak season — arrive before 2pm'. TripAdvisor 4.1/5 (71 reviews): 'best one and cheapest one on Croatian seaside'; 'manager is awesome, speaks English/Italian/Croatian'. Negatives: rocky ground ('better for camper vans than tents'); road noise on upper pitches from 6am; 160+ steep steps to beach is tiring on the way back. Taxi boat to Dubrovnik old town costs ~€15pp each way, takes 45 min — 'the best way' per staff but pricier than the bus (€2.50/person). ACSI card discount available. Campsite opens Apr 1, closes Oct 28.
cost: ~€53/night for 4 adults + small tent + car + electricity (Jun, Cat B: €8.30/adult + €5 tent + €4 car + €4 elec + €1.70/pp tourist tax). Without electricity: ~€49.
facilities: Hot showers (6), toilets (10), electricity hookups, free WiFi, laundry machines + dryer (€4/€4), private fridge boxes, grey water disposal, chemical disposal. Pebble beach via 160 steps. Bus #10 stop 200m (to Dubrovnik €2.50, last bus 23:50/01:30).
bestTime: Arrive before 2pm in peak June — fills up fast. Jun 18 evening arrival after airport pickup is fine (10 min from airport).

name: Camp Pod Maslinom (Orašac, west of Dubrovnik)
description: Terraced campsite under ancient olive trees in Orašac village, 11–12km west of Dubrovnik on the D8. 50 pitches on different levels; 350m downhill walk to a crystal-clear pebble beach. Bus stop at the entrance for €2.50 service to Dubrovnik.
communityNotes: park4night 4.53/5 (590 reviews) — one of the highest-rated camps in the Dubrovnik area. 'Very kind people. Easy access to Dubrovnik by public bus for €2.50.' 'Clean facilities, beautiful sea views from lower pitches.' 'Perfect base for Dubrovnik — bus stop literally at entrance.' Steep walk to beach (350m downhill) but 'totally worth it for crystal-clear water'. Lower pitches have sea views; quieter than Camping Kate. Open Apr 1 – Nov 10.
cost: ~€30–36/night for 2 adults + van (no elec, shoulder season); peak season ~€40–46 with electricity. 4-person tent estimate ~€44–52 in June.
facilities: Hot showers, clean toilets, WiFi (some reports unreliable), electricity hookups, laundry + dryer, drinking water, waste disposal, beach 350m downhill. Bus stop at entrance.

name: Auto Camp Matkovica (Srebreno)
description: Small campsite (~20 pitches) in Srebreno, ~9–10km south of Dubrovnik and 3 min walk from a pebble beach. Budget option with shops, restaurants and a shopping centre nearby. Good bus and water-taxi access to Dubrovnik.
communityNotes: park4night 3.95/5 (249 reviews). 'Very helpful owner arranged ferry tickets.' Facilities described as basic but clean and functional; road noise noted. Pricing ~€18–30/night for 2 people + van (no electricity); smaller and more cramped layout than Camping Kate or Pod Maslinom. Strict 11am checkout. Water taxi to Dubrovnik €15pp return. Best for groups wanting proximity to Srebreno beach over a scenic natural setting.
cost: ~€25–35/night for 4 + tent + car in June (estimate; call to confirm)
facilities: Showers, toilets, electricity, WiFi, laundry, drinking water, waste disposal. Pebble beach 3 min walk. Bus and water taxi to Dubrovnik.

name: Vruja Bay — submarine freshwater spring (Brela, 2km north of Punta Rata)
description: A secluded pebble cove where a massive submarine freshwater spring (60m diameter cave at 28m depth) erupts directly onto a sea beach — Croatia's only protected underwater spring. You swim in clear Adriatic water that turns noticeably colder and fresher near shore. No road access: reach by a 2km coastal path north from Punta Rata, or by kayak from Brela.
communityNotes: Officially protected as a natural monument. 'Snorkeling over the spring vent is surreal — you can see the cold fresh water spiralling up through the warm sea.' Uncrowded because there's no road — most tourists stick to Punta Rata. Best paired as an afternoon walk after the Punta Rata swim stop. No facilities at the cove itself (no shade, no water); bring food and water. June: sea ~22°C surface, noticeable cold upwelling near the spring.
cost: Free (2km walk from Brela)
bestTime: afternoon after Punta Rata; quiet on weekdays
